channel.title: arXiv keyword digest
channel.description: Daily arXiv papers summarized with Codex

item.title: Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper argues that both humans and LLMs show similar everyday causal reasoning failures, and that LLM attention heads tied to pattern matching can predict analogous human errors.

Problem: The paper studies whether failures in common-sense and everyday causal reasoning are unique evidence against LLM reasoning, or whether humans exhibit similar failures under comparable prompt conditions.

Method: The authors evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on everyday common-sense reasoning tasks, compare their error patterns, and identify attention heads that drive LLM responses to test whether those mechanisms resemble pattern matching.

Key contribution: Its novel claim is that specific LLM attention heads implementing pattern-matching behavior can predict seemingly inexplicable human reasoning errors triggered by irrelevant prompt details, linking human and model failures mechanistically rather than only behaviorally.

Main result: From the abstract, humans and LLMs exhibit similar reasoning error patterns, and the identified LLM attention heads help predict human errors caused by irrelevant details in prompts.

Limitation: The abstract does not report dataset size, task design details, statistical strength, model list, or whether the attention-head analysis establishes causality rather than correlation.

Relevance: This directly matches the causal reasoning feed because it examines everyday causal and common-sense reasoning, comparing human reasoning with LLM mechanisms and challenging abstract-world-model accounts.

Tags: causal reasoning, common-sense reasoning, LLMs, human cognition, attention heads, pattern matching, reasoning errors

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline figure showing the human/LLM task comparison, attention-head identification, and prediction of human errors from LLM attention patterns.

Authors: Zach Studdiford, Gary Lupyan

Query: causal reasoning, causality

PDF

item.title: Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies when chain-of-thought reasoning actually causally determines an LLM’s answer and finds a detectable commitment boundary after which many reasoning steps are epiphenomenal.

Problem: CoT is widely used for inference-time scaling, but it is unclear which individual reasoning steps causally influence the final answer and when the model’s answer becomes fixed during a reasoning trace.

Method: The authors estimate each CoT step’s causal importance using early exit, analyze answer formation across several model families and tasks, and train attention probes to decode answer-formation stages from intermediate reasoning steps.

Key contribution: The main novelty is the identification and probing of a sharp commitment boundary in CoT traces, separating transient intermediate guesses from a stable answer, plus a practical early-exit strategy based on this signal.

Main result: The abstract reports that CoT length can be reduced by up to 55% on average with negligible performance impact, and that attention probes decode answer-formation stages with high accuracy and robust generalization to unseen reasoning tasks.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the exact model families, task suite, baseline comparisons, or whether the early-exit method preserves calibration, robustness, or performance on harder out-of-distribution reasoning cases.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the causality query because it measures the causal importance of individual reasoning steps and distinguishes causally influential CoT content from post-commitment steps that do not alter the final answer probability.

Tags: causality, chain-of-thought, large language models, reasoning, early exit, interpretability, attention probes, inference-time scaling

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or trace figure showing early-exit causal importance scores over CoT steps, the detected commitment boundary, and the subsequent epiphenomenal reasoning segment.

Authors: Daniel Scalena, Sara Candussio, Luca Bortolussi, Elisabetta Fersini, Malvina Nissim, Gabriele Sarti

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes a Bayesian causal-inference framework that uses genomic information as an initial personalized prior for interpreting physiological measurements before enough individual behavioral data are available.

Problem: Personalized health AI systems suffer from a cold-start problem: without weeks of individual data, models struggle to distinguish a person's constitutional baseline from environmentally or state-driven physiological deviations.

Method: The authors propose a genomically anchored Bayesian inference scheme in which GWAS-derived effect sizes and risk-allele counts initialize an individual's physiological set-point prior, incoming measurements are interpreted as deviations from that prior, and the genomic prior gradually decays as empirical personal data accumulate.

Key contribution: The novel element is using a fixed genomic profile as an exogenous, domain-informed anchor for early personalized physiological interpretation, with evidence-graded priors, dynamic prior decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and explicit separation between attribution and deterministic prediction.

Main result: No empirical benchmark or validation result is reported in the abstract; the main demonstrated result is conceptual, illustrated by how the same HRV value can imply opposite interpretations depending on the genomic prior.

Limitation: The abstract does not report experiments, clinical validation, uncertainty calibration, or performance comparisons against standard personalization baselines; practical reliability also depends on GWAS effect validity, ancestry matching, and avoiding overinterpretation of contested genes.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the causal inference feed because it frames genomic variables as exogenous anchors, discusses association versus Mendelian randomization versus individual causal attribution, and proposes a causal-Bayesian architecture for personalized health AI.

Tags: causal inference, Bayesian inference, personalized medicine, genomics, physiological monitoring, cold start, Mendelian randomization

Figure note: A useful figure would show the pipeline from genomic profile to GWAS-weighted prior, physiological measurement deviation, Bayesian belief update, and time-decayed transition toward an empirical personal baseline.

Authors: Aruna Dey, Suraj Biswas

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: NetCause: Counterfactual Learning for Root Cause Analysis in Large-Scale Networks
item.description:
TL;DR: NetCause learns graph-temporal fault propagation patterns in large production networks and uses counterfactual simulation to rank likely root causes of customer-impacting incidents.

Problem: The paper studies root cause analysis for large-scale networks where faults propagate through complex physical and logical dependencies, making static rules, correlation heuristics, and topology-local reasoning insufficient in dynamic operational environments.

Method: It proposes NetCause, a self-supervised learning framework that represents incidents as graph-temporal processes and applies counterfactual simulation to score and rank candidate root cause hypotheses, with outputs intended to support operator mitigation and remediation workflows.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is combining learned network fault-propagation modeling with counterfactual reasoning for interpretable causal root-cause ranking, rather than relying primarily on static heuristics or local topology/correlation signals.

Main result: On over 1,500 incidents from six months of a cloud provider production network and 31 expert-labeled evaluation incidents, NetCause improves root cause ranking quality and reports a 16.1% accuracy improvement over a rule-based heuristic baseline; inference takes seconds of GPU time per incident.

Limitation: The abstract reports only 31 expert-labeled evaluation incidents, does not describe robustness across other providers or network types, and notes that training is computationally intensive.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the query 'causality' because it uses counterfactual simulation to causally attribute observed customer impact to underlying network root causes.

Tags: causality, counterfactual learning, root cause analysis, network operations, graph-temporal modeling, self-supervised learning, cloud networks

Figure note: A useful image would be a NetCause pipeline or architecture figure showing telemetry ingestion, graph-temporal incident modeling, counterfactual simulation, and ranked root-cause hypotheses.

Authors: Fabien Chraim, Jian Zhang, Dominik Janzing, Xiang Song, Christos Faloutsos, John Evans

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Graphical Causal Reasoning for Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Networks
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes a graph-based causal discovery and inference system for root cause analysis of large-scale cloud network incidents, reporting strong recall on labeled production incidents and qualitative production usefulness.

Problem: It studies how to identify root causes of incidents in complex cloud networks, where rule-based automation can struggle with scale, temporal dependencies, and dynamic operational behavior.

Method: The approach groups incident signals spatiotemporally, uses an automation ontology to reduce dimensionality, builds causal graphs from binary time-series data with bivariate Granger causality and conditional independence tests, and performs time-aware causal graph traversal using edge-specific lag-dependent conditional probabilities.

Key contribution: Its novelty is the combination of causal discovery over cloud network incident time series with dimensionality reduction via operational ontology and a probabilistic, interpretable, time-lag-aware root cause scoring procedure designed for production RCA workflows.

Main result: On 35 labeled production incidents from a major cloud provider, the system recalled the correct root cause in 85.7% of cases and produced an exact match in 74.3%; it has also reportedly been used in over 800 real-world production incidents with positive qualitative engineer feedback.

Limitation: The abstract reports only 35 labeled incidents for quantitative evaluation, does not describe baselines or statistical uncertainty, and production evidence is qualitative rather than a controlled empirical comparison.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the query 'causal discovery' because it applies causal graph construction and causal reasoning to a real operational cloud-network RCA setting rather than a purely synthetic benchmark.

Tags: causal discovery, root cause analysis, cloud networks, Granger causality, conditional independence, time series, causal graph, network operations

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or architecture figure showing spatiotemporal grouping, ontology-based dimensionality reduction, causal graph construction, and time-aware root cause traversal.

Authors: Fabien Chraim, Dominik Janzing, John Evans

Query: causal discovery, causal reasoning, causality

PDF

item.title: GeoHAT: Geometry-Adaptive Hybrid Action Transformer for Mobile Manipulation
item.description:
TL;DR: GeoHAT is a diffusion-based mobile manipulation policy that injects reliable dense 3D geometry into vision foundation model features and decodes arm/base actions through separate, attention-based subspaces.

Problem: The paper studies whole-body mobile manipulation, where a robot must coordinate a mobile base and manipulator under changing viewpoints while maintaining robust geometric perception and generating appropriate actions for both control modalities.

Method: GeoHAT uses a lightweight Fourier spatial encoder to convert dense per-pixel 3D coordinates into geometric tokens, gated fusion to selectively inject those tokens into pretrained vision features based on depth validity, and a Hybrid Whole-Body Action Decoder that separates arm and base action spaces with sparse cross-attention plus causal temporal modeling.

Key contribution: The main novelty is geometry-adaptive fusion: dense 3D information is added only where depth is reliable and only where the policy needs it, avoiding both sparse 3D representations and heavy 3D backbones while preserving pretrained semantic features. The action decoder also explicitly separates arm and base control instead of treating them as one monolithic action vector.

Main result: On the ManiSkill-HAB simulation benchmark, GeoHAT reports a 79.3% mean success rate, outperforming the strongest baseline by 23.7%; the abstract also claims consistent gains in real-world experiments across diverse tasks.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the number or type of real-world tasks, dataset scale, baseline identities, ablation details, runtime cost, or whether improvements hold under severe depth noise or out-of-distribution environments.

Relevance: This paper matched the query 'causality' because it mentions causal temporal modeling for action coordination, but it does not appear to study causality in the causal inference or causal representation learning sense. Relevance is therefore indirect unless the researcher is tracking causal/autoregressive temporal models in robotics policies.

Tags: mobile manipulation, robot learning, diffusion policy, vision-language/vision foundation features, 3D geometry, whole-body control, causal temporal modeling

Figure note: A useful figure would show the GeoHAT pipeline: RGB-D input, Fourier spatial encoding of per-pixel 3D coordinates, gated geometry fusion into vision features, and the hybrid arm/base action decoder with sparse cross-attention.

Authors: Xiangyu Zhu, Renjun Wu, Luzhou Ge, Jinyan Liu, Xuesong Li

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: MoVerse: Real-Time Video World Modeling with Panoramic Gaussian Scaffold
item.description:
TL;DR: MoVerse turns a single narrow-field-of-view image into a real-time, interactively navigable video world by combining panoramic diffusion, a persistent 3D Gaussian scaffold, and a distilled causal video renderer.

Problem: The paper studies single-image world creation for interactive roaming, where the input sees only a small part of the environment but the output must provide surrounding scene content, persistent geometry, controllable camera motion, and temporally coherent photorealistic video.

Method: MoVerse separates world construction from observation rendering: it first expands the input into a gravity-aligned 360-degree panorama using topology-aware diffusion, lifts that panorama into a dense 3D Gaussian scaffold via panoramic geometry-aware residual prediction, then renders user-controlled camera trajectories with a Gaussian-conditioned video renderer. A bidirectional diffusion teacher is distilled into a causal autoregressive student for bounded-latency streaming.

Key contribution: The novel aspect is the pipeline that closes missing scene context before 3D reasoning, stores the result as an explicit persistent Gaussian scaffold, and uses teacher-student distillation to obtain a causal renderer suitable for real-time interactive video rather than offline generation.

Main result: From the abstract, MoVerse runs real-time scene roaming at 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU; no quantitative quality comparisons or benchmark scores are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report evaluation datasets, baselines, perceptual metrics, user studies, failure cases, or how well the hallucinated 360-degree scene remains semantically correct beyond visual plausibility.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query 'causality' is limited: the paper uses a causal autoregressive student model for streaming latency, but it does not appear to study causal inference, causal representation learning, or cause-effect reasoning.

Tags: computer-vision, video-world-model, single-image-3d, panorama-generation, 3d-gaussian-splatting, diffusion-models, autoregressive-rendering, real-time-rendering

Figure note: A pipeline figure would be useful: single narrow-FOV input image -> topology-aware 360-degree panorama expansion -> 3D Gaussian scaffold construction -> Gaussian-conditioned video rendering along user camera trajectories with teacher-to-causal-student distillation.

Authors: Yang Zhou, Ziheng Wang, Yuqin Lu, Haofeng Liu, Jun Liang, Shengfeng He, Jing Li

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Navigating the Safety-Fidelity Trade-off: Massive-Variate Time Series Forecasting for Power Systems via Probabilistic Scenarios
item.description:
TL;DR: PowerPhase is a large-scale probabilistic forecasting benchmark for power-system time series, paired with PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecaster that uses type-specific heads and a causal bridge between variable groups.

Problem: The paper studies probabilistic forecasting for massive multivariate power systems where channels have different physical meanings and operational constraints, arguing that existing benchmarks are too small or do not evaluate temporal probabilistic safety constraints at scale.

Method: It introduces PowerPhase, a benchmark spanning six transmission grids with 2,000 to 36,964 jointly forecasted channels generated from AC power-flow solves, plus constraint-aware metrics such as Safety_mBrier, NECV, and CVaR-alpha. It also proposes PowerForge, a scenario-based quantile forecasting model with variable-type-specific decoding heads and a causal bridge between variable groups.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is the combination of massive-variate probabilistic power-system forecasting, physics-derived target trajectories, constraint-aware evaluation, and an explicit safety-fidelity framing showing that distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction can rank models differently.

Main result: Across eight baselines and three seeds, the abstract reports that model rankings differ between distributional accuracy and constraint satisfaction, and that PowerForge achieves the best average rank on every grid.

Limitation: The abstract does not report absolute metric values, statistical significance, details of the six grids, or evidence about generalization beyond simulated AC power-flow-derived targets.

Relevance: This paper matched the query "causality" because PowerForge includes a causal bridge between variable groups, applying causal structure ideas to probabilistic multivariate forecasting in power systems.

Tags: probabilistic forecasting, massive-variate time series, power systems, benchmark, constraint-aware evaluation, causality, quantile forecasting, safety-fidelity trade-off

Figure note: A useful image would be the PowerForge architecture or pipeline figure showing scenario generation, type-specific decoding heads, and the causal bridge between variable groups.

Authors: Kaijie Xu, Anqi Wang, Xilin Dai

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents
item.description:
TL;DR: SkillCAT is a training-free framework for LLM-agent skill self-evolution that uses contrastive causal evidence from multiple trajectories, patch replay assessment, and topology-aware skill routing to improve reusable skill documents.

Problem: The paper studies how LLM agents can convert execution trajectories into reliable reusable skills, addressing weaknesses in existing pipelines that learn from only one trajectory per task, merge unverified skill patches, and load the entire skill corpus at inference time.

Method: SkillCAT separates skill evolution into three stages: Contrastive Causal Extraction samples multiple trajectories and compares same-task success/failure pairs to find outcome-explaining evidence; Assessment-Augmented Evolution replays candidate patches on cloned source tasks and retains only patches that preserve or improve outcomes before hierarchical merging; Topology-Aware Task Execution compiles skills into a routable sub-skill topology so inference loads only task-relevant capability nodes.

Key contribution: The main novelty is combining contrastive causal extraction, outcome-preserving patch validation, and topology-aware skill retrieval into a training-free skill self-evolution pipeline, rather than relying on single-trajectory learning, unchecked merging, or full-corpus prompting.

Main result: From the abstract, SkillCAT is evaluated on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, DocVQA, plus cross-model and out-of-distribution settings, and improves average score over baselines by up to 40.40%.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify baseline names, absolute scores, statistical significance, compute or trajectory-sampling cost, or whether gains hold beyond the listed document/table/spreadsheet-style agent benchmarks.

Relevance: This is relevant to the `causality` feed because its core extraction stage explicitly compares success and failure trajectories to identify causal evidence explaining task-outcome differences, applying causal-style reasoning to LLM-agent skill evolution.

Tags: LLM agents, skill self-evolution, causal extraction, contrastive learning, agent benchmarks, training-free framework, skill retrieval

Figure note: A useful image would be the SkillCAT pipeline or architecture figure showing the three stages: Contrastive Causal Extraction, Assessment-Augmented Evolution, and Topology-Aware Task Execution.

Authors: Kunfeng Chen, Qihuang Zhong, Juhua Liu, Bo Du

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Semiparametric Bayesian inference for causal mediation in cluster randomized trials
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes a semiparametric Bayesian inference framework for causal mediation analysis in cluster randomized trials with cluster-level mediators and few clusters.

Problem: It studies how to estimate natural direct and indirect causal effects in cluster randomized trials when mediation occurs through a cluster-level mediator and standard large-sample inference gives unreliable variance estimates and confidence intervals with small numbers of clusters.

Method: The approach combines parametric Bayesian models for the outcome and mediator with a similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap that uses a distance metric between clusters to quantify uncertainty while borrowing more information from similar clusters.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element described in the abstract is the similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap for mediation inference in small-cluster CRTs, intended to avoid restrictive parametric uncertainty assumptions while improving finite-sample coverage.

Main result: Simulation studies reportedly achieve nominal coverage probability across diverse scenarios, and the method is applied to a cluster randomized trial in Kenya.

Limitation: The abstract does not report effect sizes, sample sizes, comparisons against specific competing methods, or details on how sensitive results are to the chosen cluster distance metric and causal assumptions.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the query "causality" because it develops inference methods for causal mediation, specifically estimating natural direct and indirect effects under causal assumptions in randomized clustered settings.

Tags: causal mediation, cluster randomized trials, Bayesian inference, semiparametric methods, Bayesian bootstrap, finite-sample inference, natural direct effect, natural indirect effect

Figure note: No direct image URL is available from the provided metadata. A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the CRT structure, treatment, cluster-level mediator, outcome model, and similarity-weighted Bayesian bootstrap uncertainty step.

Authors: Woojung Bae, Michael Daniels, Joseph Hogan, Rajesh Vedanthan, Stavroula Chrysanthopoulou

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories
item.description:
TL;DR: This paper causally studies whether agentic AI coding tool adoption changes software architectural quality in Java repositories, finding that smell density falls mainly because code size grows, not because smell counts improve.

Problem: The paper addresses the lack of causal evidence on whether AI coding tools, especially agentic 'vibe coding' workflows, affect architecture-level software quality rather than only code-level metrics.

Method: The authors mine 151 open-source Java repositories, identify 74 with detectable agentic AI adoption and 77 propensity-matched controls, build 1,811 monthly Arcan architectural snapshots, and estimate effects using a staggered difference-in-differences design with the Borusyak imputation estimator.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is applying a recent causal mining design for AI-tool adoption to architecture-level outcomes, while decomposing architectural smell density into raw smell counts and lines of code to show how density metrics can be misleading.

Main result: Total architectural smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82), lines of code increase substantially (+12.8%, p = 0.003), and architectural smell density declines by 6.7% (p = 0.004), implying a denominator effect rather than architectural improvement.

Limitation: The abstract supports causal identification checks but does not establish whether adoption detection captures all AI use, whether Java open-source repositories generalize to other ecosystems, or whether architectural quality beyond Arcan smell metrics changes.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the 'causality' feed because it uses a staggered difference-in-differences causal design to estimate the impact of agentic AI adoption on software architecture outcomes.

Tags: causal inference, difference-in-differences, software engineering, AI coding tools, architectural smells, mining software repositories

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's causal study pipeline or event-study figure showing agentic AI adoption timing, treated versus matched control repositories, and changes in smell counts, LOC, and smell density.

Authors: Oliver Aleksander Larsen, Mahyar T. Moghaddam

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: HYDRA-X: Native Unified Multimodal Models with Holistic Visual Tokenizers
item.description:
TL;DR: HYDRA-X proposes a unified multimodal model with a single ViT-based holistic tokenizer for both images and videos, using causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, and teacher-supervised decompression to support understanding, generation, and editing.

Problem: The paper studies how unified multimodal models can tokenize diverse visual inputs, especially images and videos, into one compact representation space without separate image/video tokenizers or inefficient full spatiotemporal processing.

Method: HYDRA-X uses a single Vision Transformer tokenizer with frame-level causal temporal attention for reconstruction, hierarchical temporal compression for video efficiency, and a lightweight decompressor trained with joint image-video teacher supervision to inject semantic awareness into the latent space. It also moves source-target interaction for editing into the tokenizer latent space rather than relying on LLM-level semantic interaction.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is a native unified image-video tokenizer inside one ViT, plus evidence that causal temporal attention and hierarchical temporal compression are better design choices than full spatiotemporal attention or single-step compression for this setting.

Main result: The abstract reports strong performance across image and video understanding and generation tasks at a 7B dense model scale, and says latent-level editing improves consistency and convergence, but it does not provide specific benchmark numbers.

Limitation: The abstract does not include quantitative results, dataset details, compute cost, comparison baselines, or evidence about robustness and scalability beyond the stated 7B instantiation.

Relevance: The match to the query 'causality' is narrow but real: the paper uses frame-level causal temporal attention as a core architectural finding for video reconstruction, though it is not about causal inference or causal reasoning.

Tags: multimodal models, visual tokenization, video tokenization, Vision Transformer, causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, image-video generation, editing

Figure note: A useful image would be the HYDRA-X architecture or tokenizer pipeline figure showing the single ViT tokenizer, causal temporal attention, hierarchical temporal compression, decompressor, and latent-level editing interaction.

Authors: Guozhen Zhang, Xuerui Qiu, Yutao Cui, Tianhui Song, Changlin Li, Junzhe Li, Tao Huang, Xiao Zhang, Yang Li, Jianbing Wu, Miles Yang, Zhao Zhong, Liefeng Bo, Limin Wang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causal invariance in graphical models with latent variables
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies when causal invariance methods for identifying causal parents remain valid in graphical models where some parent variables are latent.

Problem: Causal invariance can identify causal parents by exploiting stable effects across experimental settings, but unobserved parents can make the observed-variable graph non-DAG-like and non-unique, undermining standard causal discovery assumptions.

Method: The paper gives a graphical and statistical characterization of induced graphs over observed variables under relevant latent-parent configurations, then formalizes conditions under which invariance of causal effects is preserved for observed-parent identification.

Key contribution: It establishes formal necessary and sufficient conditions for testing causal invariance in the presence of latent parents, specifically for a multivariate Gaussian target, rather than assuming all causal parents are observed or that the observed graph remains a DAG.

Main result: The abstract reports theoretical results: characterization of induced graphs and necessary and sufficient invariance-testing conditions for a multivariate Gaussian target. No empirical benchmark or numerical result is described in the abstract.

Limitation: Evidence appears theoretical from the abstract; it does not report experiments, scalability analysis, robustness beyond the stated latent-parent configurations, or results outside the multivariate Gaussian target setting.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the 'causal discovery' feed because it addresses a core failure mode in causal parent identification: latent variables that break standard DAG-based causal discovery and invariance assumptions.

Tags: causal discovery, causal invariance, latent variables, graphical models, DAGs, Gaussian models, causal inference

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or graphical-model figure comparing the full DAG with latent parents to the induced observed-variable graph and showing when causal invariance is preserved.

Authors: Marco Borriero, Monia Lupparelli, Giovanni M. Marchetti, Veronica Vinciotti

Query: causal discovery, causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper mechanistically studies why reward models trained for both helpfulness and harmlessness can exhibit objective interference, using activation-based neuron identification and targeted ablations to test causal roles of objective-associated neurons.

Problem: Reward models used in RLHF must balance helpfulness and harmlessness, but the internal representations behind these objectives and the reasons they conflict in mixed-objective training are not well understood.

Method: The authors compare reward models trained with helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed objectives, then use activation-based methods to identify neurons associated with each objective and targeted ablations to test their functional and causal effects on model behavior.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is a mechanistic account of alignment tension: objective-associated neurons support their own objective while often suppressing the opposing one, and shared helpfulness/harmlessness neurons appear to have disproportionate behavioral influence.

Main result: From the abstract, mixed-objective reward models often underperform single-objective models, suggesting interference; ablations indicate that identified neurons causally support corresponding objectives and can negatively affect the opposing objective.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, model scales, evaluation metrics, effect sizes, or whether the findings generalize beyond the studied reward models.

Relevance: The paper is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it uses targeted neuron ablations to move beyond correlation and test causal contributions of internal activations to reward-model objectives.

Tags: RLHF, reward-models, mechanistic-interpretability, alignment, causal-ablation, helpfulness, harmlessness

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline diagram showing the three reward-model training settings, activation-based neuron selection, targeted ablation, and measured effects on helpfulness versus harmlessness.

Authors: Eshaan Tanwar, Pepa Atanasova

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: NTS-CoT proposes a chain-of-thought-based framework to reduce hallucinations and omissions in LLM-based news timeline summarization.

Problem: The paper studies hallucinations in news timeline summarization, specifically unfaithful content in news summaries and omitted information when summarizing date-event developments from rapidly changing news streams.

Method: It proposes NTS-CoT, a three-module framework: Element-CoT extracts essential news elements for faithful summarization, Date Selection combines temporal saliency with event prominence to choose timestamps, and Causal-CoT reasons about causal relationships to reduce omissions in date-event summaries.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution claimed in the abstract is a targeted treatment of hallucination types in LLM-based TLS, including causal chain-of-thought reasoning as an explicit mechanism for improving date-event coverage.

Main result: The abstract reports that NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on three TLS benchmarks and in human evaluation, improving TLS performance while mitigating hallucinations; exact metrics are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the models used, benchmark names, metric values, ablation details, or whether chain-of-thought reasoning introduces latency, cost, or interpretability risks.

Relevance: This paper matches the causality query because one core module, Causal-CoT, explicitly infers causal relationships among events to improve timeline summarization and reduce omitted event information.

Tags: timeline summarization, news summarization, LLM hallucination, chain-of-thought, causal reasoning, event causality, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful image would be the NTS-CoT pipeline diagram showing Element-CoT, Date Selection, and Causal-CoT modules and how causal reasoning feeds date-event summarization.

Authors: Feng Lyu, Huiqin Yan, Sijing Duan, Hao Wu, Shuang Gu, Xue Qiao, Weixu Zhang, Haolun Wu

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper tests whether making transformer depth routing explicit via Block Attention Residuals is enough for mechanistic interpretability, finding that exposed routing weights still require causal intervention to establish mechanism.

Problem: It studies whether inspectable cross-layer routing tensors in Block Attention Residual architectures can be interpreted as actual information-flow mechanisms, rather than merely descriptive attention-like summaries.

Method: The author compares two same-scale 0.6B Qwen3-based checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: a vanilla Qwen3 wrapped with a deterministic recency-bias routing schedule, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing included in optimization.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is a causal probe of explicit depth-routing weights, showing that architectural exposure of routing creates an interpretable target but does not by itself make routing weights mechanistically meaningful.

Main result: The wrapped baseline shows content-independent routing matching the analytic schedule, while the trained AttnRes model develops three localized routing motifs; however, high average routing mass often fails to match causal importance, and some routed source families carry mass without detectable causal effect.

Limitation: The abstract reports results only for two 0.6B checkpoints and routing-ablation interventions; it does not establish generality across model scales, tasks, architectures, or broader causal-probing methods.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the central claim depends on causal interventions distinguishing descriptive routing mass from causal contribution in neural network mechanisms.

Tags: causality, mechanistic-interpretability, transformers, routing, residual-connections, ablation, language-models

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or pipeline figure showing Block Attention Residual routing over earlier depth-source representations, alongside the causal routing-ablation setup and the three discovered routing motifs.

Authors: Aydin Javadov

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC
item.description:
TL;DR: MiniPIC is a lightweight vLLM modification for reusing KV cache entries for recurring prompt spans even when they do not appear at identical prefix positions.

Problem: The paper studies inefficient prefill in retrieval-augmented and agentic LLM workloads, where repeated structured spans such as documents or code files cannot be reused by standard prefix caching unless they share identical prefixes.

Method: It proposes Minimalistic PIC, a vLLM design that stores unrotated K vectors, applies RoPE inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes token-level cache-reuse primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator, and prompt depend.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that multiple position-independent caching schemes, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, can be expressed inside one running vLLM instance with fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, while integrating with KV cache CPU offload.

Main result: From the abstract, on 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, keeps linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and has 5.7% worst-case overhead.

Limitation: The abstract reports results on one named benchmark and does not provide evidence about broader model families, workload diversity, correctness impacts beyond caching behavior, or implementation complexity outside vLLM with the custom attention backend.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' likely because it changes effective block-level causal attention structure; it is relevant to causal masking/attention mechanics in LLM inference systems, but it does not appear to study causal inference or causality as a scientific modeling topic.

Tags: LLM inference, KV cache, vLLM, position-independent caching, RoPE, causal attention, retrieval-augmented generation, agentic workloads

Figure note: A useful image would be the MiniPIC pipeline or attention/cache architecture figure showing unrotated KV storage, per-request RoPE application, span separators, prompt dependencies, and block-level causal attention changes.

Authors: Nathan Ordonez, Thomas Parnell

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting
item.description:
TL;DR: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training module for spatio-temporal graph neural networks that learns long-horizon multi-period temporal, spatial, and cross-period causal patterns to reduce temporal mirage errors in forecasting.

Problem: The paper studies spatio-temporal forecasting under 'temporal mirage' conditions, where similar short input windows can lead to divergent futures and dissimilar windows can converge, making standard STGNNs unreliable when they rely on incomplete short-window observations.

Method: It proposes Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a pre-training plugin for existing STGNN backbones. MP3 learns multi-period temporal patterns with edge convolution, captures heterogeneous global spatial correlations using a bottleneck projection and global memory bank, and models dependencies across periods with a causality-enhanced Transformer.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is framing temporal mirage as a multi-period observation and cross-period causality problem, then addressing it with a backbone-agnostic pre-training plugin rather than a replacement forecasting architecture.

Main result: Across five STGNN baselines and five real-world datasets, including a large-scale CA dataset, MP3 reportedly gives consistent improvements, reducing MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0% on average.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the exact datasets, evaluation protocols, ablations, computational overhead, or whether the causality-enhanced Transformer identifies causal structure beyond improving predictive performance.

Relevance: The paper matches the 'causality' query because cross-period superposition causality is central to its diagnosis of temporal mirages, and the proposed framework explicitly includes a causality-enhanced Transformer for cross-period dependency modeling.

Tags: spatio-temporal forecasting, STGNN, pre-training, multi-period patterns, causality, time series, graph neural networks

Figure note: A useful image would show the MP3 pipeline: long time-series inputs split into multi-period patterns, temporal edge-convolution modeling, global spatial memory-bank modeling, causality-enhanced cross-period Transformer interaction, and integration into an STGNN backbone.

Authors: Lilan Peng, Yandi Liu, Qingren Yao, Chongshou Li, Tianrui Li

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning
item.description:
TL;DR: SWITCH makes latent chain-of-thought reasoning trainable with on-policy RL and more causally interpretable by using explicit discrete tokens to enter and exit hidden-state recurrence.

Problem: The paper studies how to make latent chain-of-thought methods based on hidden-state recurrence easier to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning and easier to analyze mechanistically or causally.

Method: It proposes SWITCH, where a model emits <swi> to enter latent reasoning mode and </swi> to exit, trains with a visible-to-latent curriculum, and uses a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation.

Key contribution: The novel idea is to use ordinary discrete boundary tokens as both RL-compatible decision anchors and intervention/probing points for mechanistic analysis of latent reasoning steps.

Main result: According to the abstract, SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale, and boundary-token analysis suggests the entry transition performs problem-specific, causally important computation.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, quantitative gains, model scale details, or whether the mechanistic findings generalize beyond the tested tasks and training setup.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper explicitly studies causal intervention and mechanistic analysis of latent reasoning, including identifying causally important hidden-state transitions.

Tags: latent reasoning, chain-of-thought, reinforcement learning, GRPO, mechanistic interpretability, causal intervention, language models

Figure note: A useful image would be the SWITCH pipeline or architecture diagram showing visible reasoning, <swi>/</swi> boundary tokens, recurrent latent steps, GRPO training, and causal probes at the boundary transitions.

Authors: Jiayu Yang, Chao Chen, Shengen Wu, Yinhong Liu, Yuxuan Fan, Lujundong Li, Songning Lai, Chengwei Qin, Zhijiang Guo

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts
item.description:
TL;DR: CausalMoE proposes a billion-scale multimodal Mixture-of-Experts foundation model for Granger causal discovery that routes time-series patches by latent temporal patterns to improve causal graph recovery under regime shifts.

Problem: The paper studies Granger causal discovery for real-world time series where distribution shifts, dynamic regimes, and heterogeneous temporal patterns cause standard neural methods to learn entangled representations and spurious causal graphs.

Method: It proposes CausalMoE, combining pattern-routed heterogeneous experts for patch-level temporal heterogeneity, causality-aware self-attention across variables for sparse graph recovery via proximal optimization, and multimodal alignment with LLM/VLM priors to regularize causal estimation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly routes temporal patches to specialized experts and integrates textual and visual priors, rather than using a single shared neural causal discovery model for all regimes.

Main result: The abstract reports new state-of-the-art results on fully supervised benchmarks and improved generalization in few-shot settings where traditional methods fail, but it does not provide quantitative metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify benchmark names, effect sizes, ablation evidence, computational cost, or whether multimodal priors improve causal validity beyond benchmark performance.

Relevance: This directly matches the configured query 'causal discovery' because it targets Granger causal graph recovery and proposes a new neural framework for causality in temporal systems.

Tags: causal discovery, Granger causality, time series, mixture of experts, foundation model, multimodal learning, few-shot generalization

Figure note: A useful image would be the model pipeline showing patch-level pattern routing, heterogeneous experts, causality-aware variable attention, and LLM/VLM prior alignment for causal graph recovery.

Authors: Bo Liu, Di Dai, Jingwei Liu, Jiarui Jin, Xiaocheng Fang, Guangkun Nie, Hongyan Li, Shenda Hong

Query: causal discovery, causality

PDF

item.title: ShrinkageTrees: An R Package for Bayesian Tree Ensembles for Survival Analysis and Causal Inference
item.description:
TL;DR: ShrinkageTrees introduces an R package implementing regularized Bayesian additive regression tree ensembles for censored survival outcomes and causal effect estimation.

Problem: The paper addresses flexible survival modeling and causal inference when outcomes are right- or interval-censored, especially in high-dimensional covariate settings where standard Bayesian tree ensembles may overfit or lack suitable regularization.

Method: It implements Bayesian additive regression tree models in an accelerated failure time framework, with optional decomposition into prognostic and treatment-effect components, using depth-penalizing and Dirichlet splitting priors for tree structure plus global-local shrinkage priors for step heights.

Key contribution: The abstract claims the first implementation of the Horseshoe Forest, which applies a horseshoe prior to tree step heights, and packages this with survival and causal inference workflows in R.

Main result: No empirical benchmark or numerical result is reported in the abstract; the stated result is software capability, including fitting, prediction, causal effect estimation, convergence diagnostics, Rcpp acceleration, and multi-chain MCMC.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide evidence on accuracy, calibration, runtime, scalability, or comparisons against existing survival or causal inference methods.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it provides Bayesian tree ensemble tools for estimating treatment effects, particularly with censored survival outcomes and high-dimensional covariates.

Tags: causal inference, survival analysis, Bayesian additive regression trees, R package, censored outcomes, Horseshoe Forest, MCMC

Figure note: A useful image would be a workflow or architecture diagram showing censored survival data entering the AFT Bayesian tree ensemble, the prognostic/treatment-effect decomposition, regularization priors, and outputs for prediction and causal effect estimation.

Authors: Tijn Jacobs

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: The Impossibility of Eliciting Latent Knowledge
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper formalizes eliciting latent knowledge in AI systems using Causal Influence Diagrams and proves that behavior-only feedback cannot guarantee an honest agent, even with perfect training feedback.

Problem: It studies how to train advanced AI systems to honestly report their beliefs about latent environmental variables that humans cannot directly observe, a problem known as eliciting latent knowledge (ELK).

Method: The authors use Causal Influence Diagrams to model the relationship between an agent's training environment, its subjective world representation, observable variables, latent variables, honesty, and goal misgeneralisation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a formal CID-based treatment of ELK that distinguishes honest reporting from merely producing answers humans would judge as true, plus an impossibility theorem for behavior-only feedback strategies.

Main result: The abstract states an impossibility theorem: no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behavior can guarantee production of an honest agent with certainty, even when training feedback is perfect.

Limitation: The abstract does not report empirical experiments, benchmarks, or practical mitigation evidence; the result appears theoretical and applies under the paper's stated formal assumptions.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper uses Causal Influence Diagrams as the core formal framework for reasoning about hidden variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and causal structure in AI alignment.

Tags: causality, causal influence diagrams, AI alignment, eliciting latent knowledge, honesty, goal misgeneralisation, theoretical AI safety

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's CID framework diagram showing observable variables, latent variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and behavior in the training environment.

Authors: Korbinian Friedl, Francis Rhys Ward, Paul Yushin Rapoport, Tom Everitt, Jonathan Richens

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Making Foresight Actionable: Repurposing Representation Alignment in World Action Models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies why visually plausible World Action Model rollouts can still yield poor robot actions and proposes AGRA, a representation-alignment objective that makes video diffusion features more useful for action decoding.

Problem: World Action Models for robot manipulation use video generation to predict future scene evolution before choosing actions, but the abstract reports that plausible generated futures do not reliably produce accurate low-level control actions.

Method: The authors diagnose the issue with action-head attention analysis and causal interventions, then introduce AGRA: an Action-Grounded Representation Alignment objective that aligns intermediate video diffusion features with spatially coherent semantic representations from a foundation visual encoder.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is reframing the world-model-to-action interface as a representation mismatch problem: hidden states optimized for visual reconstruction are regularized to become spatially and semantically organized for action control, rather than assuming better video prediction directly implies better actions.

Main result: On real-world manipulation tasks, AGRA reportedly improves attention to task-relevant interaction regions, object localization, affordance understanding, robustness to task-irrelevant perturbations, in-distribution performance, and out-of-distribution generalization over a baseline World Action Model.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, task suite details, baseline names beyond a baseline WAM, or evidence about computational cost and scalability across robot platforms.

Relevance: This is relevant to the `causality` feed because the paper explicitly uses causal interventions to identify why action decoders fail under task-irrelevant perturbations, though the main contribution is representation alignment for robot world-action models rather than a general causal inference method.

Tags: robot manipulation, world action models, video diffusion, representation alignment, causal interventions, attention analysis, affordance learning, out-of-distribution generalization

Figure note: A useful image would show the AGRA pipeline: video diffusion world-model features feeding an action decoder, the semantic foundation-encoder alignment objective, and attention or causal-intervention maps highlighting task-relevant versus task-irrelevant regions.

Authors: Lu Qiu, Yizhuo Li, Yi Chen, Yuying Ge, Yixiao Ge, Xihui Liu

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Attention by Synchronization in Coupled Oscillator Networks
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes replacing softmax attention with a Kuramoto synchronization-based oscillator mechanism designed for energy-constrained physical hardware.

Problem: It studies how to implement transformer-style attention on physical substrates where exponentiation and global reductions used by softmax are energetically expensive and physically unnatural.

Method: The proposed fixed-query oscillator attention uses learned query anchors fixed on a sphere while free oscillators evolve under Kuramoto-Lohe dynamics until equilibrium; attention weights are then encoded through cosine similarity with an affine normalization at readout.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a mathematically grounded attention operation implemented by oscillator synchronization dynamics, with a provably unique and almost globally attractive fixed point across physical realizations, avoiding exponentiation during computation.

Main result: Empirically, at oscillator dimension 2 it outperforms softmax on keyword spotting by +1.00 percentage point and subject-verb agreement by +5.27 percentage points on hard sentences; on causal language modeling, softmax remains better, but the perplexity gap shrinks as oscillator dimension increases.

Limitation: The abstract frames the work mainly as a hardware blueprint rather than a software replacement for softmax, and reports remaining gaps on causal language modeling; it does not provide hardware energy measurements or physical-device experiments in the abstract.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query 'causality' appears weak and indirect: the abstract mentions causal language modeling, but the paper is about physical implementations of attention, not causal inference or causality methods.

Tags: oscillator-attention, kuramoto-dynamics, physical-ai-hardware, transformer-attention, energy-efficient-computing, neuromorphic-computing

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing fixed query anchors on the sphere, free oscillator equilibration under Kuramoto-Lohe dynamics, and readout of attention weights via cosine similarity.

Authors: Fabio Pasqualetti, Taosha Guo

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: WorldReasoner: Evaluating Whether Language Model Agents Forecast Events with Valid Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: WorldReasoner is a benchmark framework for testing whether language-model agents can make temporally valid event forecasts using grounded evidence and causal reasoning rather than memorized facts or unsupported explanations.

Problem: The paper studies how to evaluate real-world event forecasting by language-model agents under uncertainty, especially when final-answer accuracy alone cannot distinguish valid forecasting from memorization, fabricated citations, or post hoc causal storytelling.

Method: It proposes WorldReasoner, an evaluation framework where each task provides a resolved forecasting question, a simulated forecast date, and access only to pre-date evidence; agents submit probabilities, cited evidence, and optionally causal event graphs, which are scored on outcome quality, evidence quality, and reasoning quality against hindsight reference graphs.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is a temporally constrained forecasting benchmark that evaluates not just prediction accuracy but also source grounding and causal-event reasoning, built at scale through an agentic pipeline that generates questions, gathers timestamped evidence, and constructs hindsight event graphs.

Main result: On 345 resolved tasks derived from 14,141 articles and 8,087 extracted graph events, temporally valid retrieval was the strongest driver of forecast accuracy; causal graph construction improved key-event recovery, but agents still struggled to turn grounded evidence into well-calibrated probabilities.

Limitation: The abstract does not report detailed model identities, statistical significance, human validation quality for the hindsight graphs, or whether results generalize beyond the 345 constructed forecasting tasks.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because it evaluates whether LM agents can use causal event graphs and key-event structure to support real-world forecasts, rather than relying only on surface evidence or outcome accuracy.

Tags: causality, event forecasting, language model agents, evaluation benchmark, temporal validity, retrieval, causal event graphs

Figure note: A useful image would show the WorldReasoner evaluation pipeline: forecasting question generation, timestamped evidence retrieval, agent probability and citation submission, optional causal graph construction, and the three scoring axes.

Authors: Yizhou Chi, Eric Chamoun, Zifeng Ding, Andreas Vlachos

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: AnchorEdit: Maintaining Temporal Consistency in Multi-turn Image Editing via Causal Memory
item.description:
TL;DR: AnchorEdit proposes an autoregressive diffusion framework with causal memory to reduce identity drift and accumulated errors in long-horizon multi-turn image editing.

Problem: The paper studies high-resolution iterative image editing, where models must preserve subject identity and follow instructions over many successive edits but often suffer from identity drift and error accumulation.

Method: It proposes AnchorEdit, an autoregressive diffusion-based multi-turn editing framework trained with a three-stage curriculum: identity-preserving single-turn pretraining, causal autoregressive forcing fine-tuning using self-rollout to reduce exposure bias, and consistency distillation for efficient 4-step generation; at inference, a memory mechanism anchors the initial subject identity across editing rounds.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is reframing multi-turn image editing as causal autoregressive inference rather than relying on bidirectional video-prior attention, plus adding a causal memory mechanism and a high-resolution benchmark for long-horizon editing stability.

Main result: According to the abstract, AnchorEdit achieves state-of-the-art results, preserving subject fidelity and instruction following over more than 10 interaction rounds.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative metrics, dataset details, baseline names, ablations, or evidence about generalization beyond the proposed benchmark.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' mainly through its use of causal autoregressive inference and memory for sequential generation; it is not clearly about causal effect estimation or causal discovery.

Tags: multi-turn image editing, autoregressive diffusion, causal memory, identity preservation, long-horizon consistency, computer vision

Figure note: A useful image would be the AnchorEdit pipeline or architecture figure showing the three-stage training curriculum and inference-time causal memory mechanism.

Authors: Hang Xu, Xiaoxiao Ma, Guohui Zhang, Yu Hu, Siming Fu, Jie Huang, Lin Song, Haoyang Huang, Nan Duan, Feng Zhao

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: From Prompts to Tokens: Internalizing Causal Supervision in Vision-Language Model for Multi-Image Causal Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: BridgeVLM proposes to improve multi-image visual causal reasoning by turning induced causal graphs into internal causal tokens processed inside a VLM decoder rather than relying on prompt-level causal instructions.

Problem: The paper studies brittle causal reasoning in large vision-language models, especially identifying causal variables from multi-image visual inputs and answering interventional or counterfactual queries about physical scenes.

Method: It proposes BridgeVLM, which induces a causal graph from multi-image inputs, converts that graph into structured Causal Tokens, and executes them through RAMP layers inserted into the LLM decoder for causal message passing; it also introduces M3S, a unified training interface for local and global causal supervision.

Key contribution: The main novelty is internalizing causal supervision into model execution via causal tokens and decoder-level message passing, instead of keeping causal knowledge external as text prompts.

Main result: From the abstract, BridgeVLM reports 54.4% accuracy on CausalVLBench intervention tasks versus 33.2% with prompt-level supervision, improves Causal3D from 43.6% to 49.0%, and raises CausalVLBench causal structure learning F1 from 33.4% to 75.1%.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify model scale, dataset breadth beyond CausalVLBench and Causal3D, robustness to real-world visual distributions, computational overhead from RAMP layers, or whether improvements generalize outside benchmark-style causal tasks.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because it targets visual causal reasoning, causal structure learning, interventions, and counterfactual reasoning in VLMs.

Tags: visual causal reasoning, vision-language models, causal structure learning, interventions, counterfactual reasoning, multi-image reasoning

Figure note: A useful image would be the BridgeVLM architecture or pipeline figure showing multi-image inputs, induced causal graph, Causal Token construction, and RAMP-layer message passing inside the LLM decoder.

Authors: Haoping Yu, Yuanxi Li, Jing Ma

Query: causal reasoning, causal structure learning, causality

PDF

item.title: Bracketing Relationships of Weighted Average Treatment Effects
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper derives theoretical bracketing relationships among weighted average treatment effects in observational causal inference, especially showing when the overlap-weight estimand lies between ATT and ATC.

Problem: It studies how different weighted average treatment effect estimands relate to each other in observational studies, where treatment effect heterogeneity and propensity-score-based weighting can make causal estimands difficult to compare.

Method: The authors prove theoretical bounds under a monotonic relationship between the propensity score and the conditional average treatment effect, then extend the argument to weighted local average treatment effects with a binary instrumental variable and binary treatment, as well as to other weighting schemes.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a bracketing result: under stated monotonicity conditions, the overlap-weight average treatment effect is bounded between the average treatment effects on treated and control units, giving an interpretable ordering of common causal estimands.

Main result: From the abstract, the primary result is theoretical rather than empirical: the overlap-weight estimand is bounded between ATT and ATC under the monotonic propensity-score/CATE relationship, with analogous extensions to weighted LATE settings.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data validation, or robustness evidence; the results appear to depend on monotonicity assumptions whose empirical plausibility may need diagnostic assessment.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it clarifies relationships among treatment-effect estimands used in observational causal studies and proposes a CP-plot diagnostic of estimated CATE versus estimated propensity score.

Tags: causal inference, observational studies, weighted average treatment effects, overlap weights, ATT, ATC, LATE, propensity score

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or diagnostic figure showing the proposed CP-plot: estimated conditional average treatment effect on one axis and estimated propensity score on the other, with regions illustrating the bracketing relationship among ATT, ATC, and overlap-weight ATE.

Authors: Pengfei Tian, Fan Yang, Peng Ding

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: Substrate Asymmetry in User-Side Memory: A Diagnostic Framework
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper argues that user-side LLM memory should be evaluated along separate behavioral and factual axes because parametric user adapters and retrieval systems fail in opposite ways.

Problem: It studies how to evaluate and choose memory substrates for personalized LLMs, showing that aggregate personalization scores obscure distinct failures in style consistency, factual recall, and abstention when facts are absent.

Method: The paper proposes a diagnostic framework that factorizes memory into behavioral consistency, factual presence, and factual absence, then compares per-user gamma-LoRA adapters against BGE-large top-K retrieval on a controlled 50-user synthetic corpus and a LaMP-3 real-data probe, with causal intervention by zeroing specific LoRA attention weights.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is identifying substrate asymmetry: LoRA-based parametric memory and RAG specialize in different memory axes, and the same query-projection attention cells in layers 21-35 appear causally load-bearing for opposite factual-presence and factual-absence effects.

Main result: Gamma-LoRA decisively improves behavioral style, while RAG decisively improves factual absence. Zeroing the identified LoRA weights raises absence-probe TPR by 33 percentage points but drops presence-probe TPR by 20 points. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, alignment appears to worsen the asymmetry; on LaMP-3, gamma-LoRA underperforms a majority baseline unless evaluation-time logit masking is used.

Limitation: The abstract reports a controlled synthetic corpus of only 50 users and a LaMP-3 probe; broader real-world validation, robustness across more tasks/models, and details of the causal intervention methodology are not available from the abstract.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper makes causal claims about specific LoRA attention query-projection weights load-bearing for memory behavior, using weight-zeroing interventions to test their effects.

Tags: LLM memory, personalization, LoRA, RAG, causal intervention, attention analysis, evaluation framework, alignment tax

Figure note: A useful image would show the diagnostic framework with the three memory axes, the compared substrates, and the causal intervention on attention query-projection cells in layers 21-35.

Authors: Youwang Deng

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Organize then Retrieve: Hierarchical Memory Navigation for Efficient Agents
item.description:
TL;DR: HORMA proposes a hierarchical file-system-like working memory for LLM agents that preserves raw trajectories while enabling efficient navigation-based retrieval for long-horizon tasks.

Problem: The paper studies how LLM agents can maintain useful working memory over long-horizon, multi-step tasks without relying on ever-growing contexts that degrade reasoning quality, increase cost, and add latency.

Method: HORMA builds a hierarchical memory structure from agent experience, linking summarized entities to raw trajectories, then uses a lightweight reinforcement-learning-trained navigation agent to traverse the hierarchy and retrieve minimal sufficient context for the current task.

Key contribution: The novelty is an organize-then-retrieve memory framework that explicitly preserves temporal structure and causal dependencies by connecting summaries to raw experience, instead of depending only on lossy compression or similarity-based retrieval.

Main result: From the abstract, HORMA improves task performance under constrained context budgets across ALFWorld, LoCoMo, and LongMemEval, using at most 22.17% of baseline token usage in long conversation tasks while improving efficiency-performance trade-offs.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify exact accuracy gains, ablation results, training cost for the retrieval agent, or whether the method scales to very large real-world agent histories beyond the evaluated benchmarks.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it targets memory retrieval methods that preserve temporal structure and causal dependencies needed for multi-step agent behavior.

Tags: LLM agents, working memory, hierarchical retrieval, long-horizon tasks, causal dependencies, reinforcement learning, agent memory

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or architecture diagram showing HORMA's two stages: hierarchical memory construction linking summaries to raw trajectories, followed by navigation-based retrieval through the memory tree.

Authors: Hao-Lun Hsu, Nikki Lijing Kuang, Boyi Liu, Zhewei Yao, Yuxiong He

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causal Ensemble Agent: Hierarchical Causal Discovery with LLM-guided Expert Reweighting
item.description:
TL;DR: CEA is a causal discovery framework that ensembles multiple statistical discovery experts and uses an LLM as a data-aware meta-referee to improve causal graph construction from observational data.

Problem: The paper studies how to recover accurate causal graphs from observational data when standard causal discovery algorithms disagree and when direct LLM-based causal judgments are unreliable or insufficiently aligned with the observed data.

Method: It proposes Causal Ensemble Agent (CEA), which aggregates structural outputs from multiple statistical causal discovery experts at different graph levels using linear opinion pooling, then invokes an LLM meta-referee to dynamically reweight experts when pooled confidence is near a decision boundary.

Key contribution: The genuinely new aspect is the hybrid ensemble design: instead of asking an LLM to directly infer causal edges, CEA uses the LLM selectively for meta-analysis and expert reweighting, combining statistical evidence with domain-level reasoning from feature descriptions.

Main result: According to the abstract, extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that CEA achieves the strongest overall performance across a wide range of causal discovery methods.

Limitation: The abstract does not report specific metrics, datasets, baselines, effect sizes, computational cost, or ablations, so the strength and robustness of the empirical evidence cannot be assessed from the abstract alone.

Relevance: This directly matches the configured query "causal discovery" because it proposes a new framework for observational causal graph learning and addresses disagreement among causal discovery algorithms, a central issue in the field.

Tags: causal discovery, observational data, LLM, ensemble learning, expert reweighting, causal graphs

Figure note: A useful image would be the CEA pipeline or architecture diagram showing statistical causal discovery experts, linear opinion pooling, decision-boundary detection, LLM-guided expert reweighting, and final graph composition.

Authors: Xinyu Li, Yuanyuan Wang, Haoxuan Li, Chuan Zhou, Erdun Gao, Bo Han, Tongliang Liu, Kun Zhang, Howard Bondell, Mingming Gong

Query: causal discovery, causality

PDF

item.title: HiMem-WAM: Hierarchical Memory-Gated World Action Models for Robotic Manipulation
item.description:
TL;DR: HiMem-WAM proposes a hierarchical memory-gated World Action Model for long-horizon robotic manipulation, using skill-boundary-triggered memory updates to improve task-relevant causal state tracking.

Problem: The paper studies why existing World Action Models struggle with task-relevant memory in long-horizon robotic manipulation, especially when manipulation requires remembering compact task state across extended sequences.

Method: It introduces HiMem-WAM, which combines motion-centric low-level latent actions, high-level skill latents, and a boundary-aware memory gate that writes compact task states at predicted skill transitions.

Key contribution: The novel element is the hierarchical latent action structure plus boundary-triggered memory mechanism, intended to provide temporal abstraction and memory updates without requiring test-time future video generation or optical flow estimation.

Main result: According to the abstract, evaluations on LIBERO, LIBERO-PLUS, RMBench, and real-world tasks show improved robustness under deployment perturbations from hierarchical latents and substantial gains on memory-dependent long-horizon manipulation from the memory module.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, baseline comparisons, ablation details, or enough evidence to judge how broadly the approach generalizes beyond the named benchmarks and real-world tasks.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causal inference' because it frames the memory gate as enabling causal inference over compact task states during robotic manipulation, though the causal-inference contribution appears embedded in an embodied-control architecture rather than presented as a general causal methodology.

Tags: robotics, world action models, long-horizon manipulation, hierarchical latent actions, memory, causal inference, embodied intelligence

Figure note: A framework figure showing the hierarchical low-level motion latents, high-level skill latents, predicted skill boundaries, and memory-gated state updates would be useful, but no direct image URL is available from the provided metadata.

Authors: Xiaoquan Sun, Ruijian Zhang, Chen Cao, Yihan Sun, Jiahui Chen, Zetian Xu, Bo Chen, Haijier Chen, Zhen Yang, Jiarun Zhu, Yijun Hong, JingZhe Xu, Jingrui Pang, Mingqi Yuan, Jiayu Chen

Query: causal inference

PDF

item.title: Correlation Is Not Enough: Embedding Human Metadata for Individual Causal Discovery
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper argues that biomedical sentence embeddings can create false causal evidence for Large Behavioural Models and proposes contrastive fine-tuning plus BODHI hard-negative mining to improve cross-domain separation for individual causal discovery.

Problem: Off-the-shelf biomedical encoders assign high similarity to unrelated cross-domain concepts, which is especially harmful when embeddings are used as evidence for causal edges in a graph of a person's life rather than only as noisy retrieval signals.

Method: The authors fine-tune PubMedBERT with a contrastive pass over 72,034 pairs, then apply BODHI, a second pass that mines hard negatives from biomedical knowledge-graph non-edges. They also benchmark serving performance with OpenVINO on Intel Xeon hardware with and without AMX.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that embedding geometry is a correctness condition for individual causal discovery in Large Behavioural Models, and that knowledge-graph-derived absent edges can be used as hard negatives to reduce false cross-domain proximity.

Main result: From the abstract, contrastive training improves PubMedBERT BIOSSES correlation from 0.633 to 0.828 and within-vs-across-domain separation from 1.05x to 1.63x. BODHI further raises separation to 2.30x and the discrimination gap to +0.392, with a 4.5% BIOSSES cost. OpenVINO reduces single-query latency from 1367 ms to 10 ms on an AMX-enabled Xeon 6737P.

Limitation: The abstract does not report validation on real downstream individual causal-discovery outcomes, only embedding discrimination, BIOSSES correlation, and serving benchmarks. It is also unclear how broadly BODHI generalizes beyond the chosen biomedical knowledge graph and encoders.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query "causal discovery" because it studies how embedding errors can induce false causal links in person-centered causal graphs and proposes a training framework to reduce those errors.

Tags: causal discovery, biomedical embeddings, contrastive learning, hard negatives, knowledge graphs, Large Behavioural Models, PubMedBERT, OpenVINO

Figure note: A useful image would show the proposed pipeline: pretrained biomedical encoder, contrastive pair training, BODHI knowledge-graph hard-negative mining, and downstream causal-edge construction in a personal life graph.

Authors: Suraj Biswas, Saurabh Gupta, Pritam Mukherjee

Query: causal discovery

PDF

item.title: Closing the Prior-Posterior Loop: Self-Reflective Molecular Design with Analysis-Driven LLM Iteration
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes a self-reflective LLM framework for molecular design that feeds detailed first-principles physicochemical analyses back into the generation loop to support more mechanistic causal reasoning than scalar score feedback.

Problem: It studies whether general-purpose LLMs can design molecules to satisfy target properties with chemist-like precision, rather than relying on generate-score-reject loops that provide only scalar feedback.

Method: The framework couples retrieval-augmented generation with a self-reflection module that returns orbital energies, atomic charges, electron densities, and related structure-property rationale from first-principles calculations to the LLM during iterative molecular design.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is replacing compressed scalar rewards or scores with rich physicochemical explanations, allowing the LLM to reason about why candidate molecules fail and how structural changes should affect target properties.

Main result: From the abstract, on HOMO-LUMO gap targets from 1.0 to 5.0 eV, the SPR reflection approach reaches deviations as low as 0.0003 eV and reports 100% success on moderate tasks, outperforming scalar-feedback and non-reflective baselines; it also generalizes to dipole-moment design and across five LLM backbones.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify dataset size, molecule validity constraints, computational cost of first-principles feedback, benchmark breadth beyond the named properties, or whether success holds for harder real-world medicinal or materials design objectives.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because the paper explicitly frames detailed physicochemical feedback as a way to turn LLM molecular optimization from stochastic trial-and-error into mechanistic, cause-aware iteration.

Tags: causal reasoning, molecular design, large language models, self-reflection, retrieval-augmented generation, computational chemistry, structure-property relationships

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline diagram showing the prior-posterior loop: LLM/RAG molecule proposal, first-principles calculation, extraction of orbital energies/charges/electron densities, SPR reflection, and revised molecule generation.

Authors: Junyi Gong, Zijie Qiu, Ben Zhong Tang

Query: causal reasoning

PDF
